speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Max Sitzer. This is June 30, 1995. We're in Toronto. Mr. Sitzer, if you can tell us a little bit more about your experience in the village of your, your girlfriend, where you were confronted with the Ukrainian and the German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That [? Wygoda-- ?] how I came to that [? Wygoda ?] is-- it was a Judenrat. Judenrat was the Germans create-- they didn't want to deal with the individual Jews. So they create a like a little, a little government or whatever, a Jewish-- and, and they select a few Jews. And through them, it's just like a city-- like a city hall or, or you know? So in that particular [? Wygoda, ?] I met my girlfriend. And that's how-- in that [? Wygoda, ?] I think, was living only one, two, two Jews, and a big landowner. Was three Jew-- three Jewish families. All [? Wygoda ?] was, was about maybe, maybe 30 families. So three of them was-- were Jewish. I knew that Kranc. And then was another family-- I don't remember. And then was a, a landowner, which I don't know. I don't know his name. I forgot. And we used to-- once in a while, we used to go there for different things. Like they called us in if they need workers or they need, they need something. Or sometimes, the German demanded something through them, we should contribute. I remember in '41, they demand furs. They needed furs for the military. So the Jew-- they-- I remember they gave an order that the Jews are not allowed to have in possession any furs. So that Judenrat let us know that all the furs we have, we should deliver to there. And they'll deliver to the Germans. That kind of stuff-- that's how I knew [? Wygoda. ?] But before that, I never knew that [? Wygoda ?] exists even. And with the girlfriend-- that's when I met my girlfriend. And that's when I was with her-- maybe '41, not quite a year, not quite a year. And she died in front of me. I seeing it. So is the whole family. Now, I didn't finish that when I ran from the Germans-- so I ran, Josi Kranc ran, and Dowcze Apelfeld ran. Three of us ran. Dowcze Apelfeld got killed later by Ukrainian. Josi went back home, met his father, and his sister, and his sister boyfriend or his sister's future husband. And when I came in to the house-- no. Before, before I went to their house, I went to a farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I seen a light. I was very hungry. Came in, and I see and sitting him and eat. And I says, can I have something to eat? I have two zlotys. That was occupation money. So I says, can I-- can you sell me something? He says, I got nothing, he said, bread and potatoes. I say, that's fine. So he let me have it. I took out the two zlotys and gave it to him. Then he tells me, he says, you know what happened today? I say, no. I pretend that I was going from one village to Strzemilcze. He says, today, all the Krances got shot. But one guy from Strzemilcze ran. He didn't know that he's talking to me. And I was the one that I ran. I said, I didn't know. I didn't hear nothing. So after I left him, I went to the barn and lay down in the barn. Because I was afraid to go back to the Kranc's house. I laid out to the barn. And I, I took out one board in case somebody came in the back. And somebody came in through the door, so I'll run in the back. Laid down, I couldn't fall asleep. And finally I said, I'm going there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came in to the Kranc's-- his, the father, the-- one daughter, the future husband, and that-- your little Josi, 10 years, they were sitting and looking on pictures. I remember. I knew the house very well. I opened the oven. And I knew there's something there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took out-- it either was a duck or a chicken. And you know, I was so hungry, I just couldn't-- and I ate. And then Kranc says, let's go and look at the-- at, at-- I want to see my family. We went there. And they were all laying down there, not buried, shot in the back. So after-- and it start getting early in the morning. Kranc said, Max, I have a place where to go. I can't take you. I says, I know where you're going. That's my last word. That's my last time I see him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, I don't want to go to that place. And I was right, because they found them there, murdered, all choked they were with a wire-- him, and his daughter, and the son, and the, and the future, and the future-- the daughter's husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to a Ukrainian family, which I knew the family. Because I knew they were friends before the war. Then, when we were hiding already, this particular family was-- which hid us came over one morning. And they told us-- and he told us that they found the Krances all dead on a field. He killed them. He killed them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, when he told me that, because is going there. So I remember that my father told me that he is looking for a place to hide in, in such and such a little village. That village was named Uvin-- U-V-I-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but I did not know the house. So when I left that place where they killed all those-- the Krances family, it start getting daytime. So after the First World War was bunkes left. They called the [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got in one of those bunkers, stayed over day-- over the daytime, because I couldn't, I couldn't walk in the daytime. It was very dangerous. The Ukrainian was killing that time, yeah. And I got into the bunkers, stood over the, over the daytime. And at night, I went to that village, Uvin. Seeing one house lit up-- it's a small village-- I walked. I knocked on the door. And I says, can I have a drink of water? It's a woman by the name Helen. She says, you looking for somebody? I say, I'm not looking for nobody. I'm going to Strzemilcze. Oh, to Strzemilcze? She says, I know somebody from Strzemilcze. She was the girlfriend-- not the wife-- from the guy which hid us. She was the girlfriend. I didn't know it at the time, but I found out it.

speaker: interviewee
text: She took me, took me right there to this. And she says, here is the house. And that's your-- he is your father. You know, I start talking to her. I see she knew all about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So OK, so that particular house-- they guys how-- the guy who hid us was Wolszyn-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] W-O-L-S-Z-Y-N-- his name. And his first name was Jakob. They called him [? Yatsko, ?] but they called him Jacob Wolszyn. He was about-- not quite five, maybe four feet, not quite five-- very short.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before the war, he was working for a big landowner. Was a drunkard, a thief, liked to play cards, had a girlfriend, belonged to a nationalistic organization called Bandera. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he-- my father met him accidentally a long time ago. And he-- when he approached him, he said, look, we want a place. He says, I'll, I'll do it. For the money, I'll do it. But my father didn't have any money. Because when we ran from Strzemilcze, we didn't take nothing with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father went to a city called Radziechow-- R-A-D-Z-I-E-C-H-O-W, Radziechow-- went there. There was already a ghetto-- without me-- and said to some guys he knew. He says, I got the place. You got the money? We got a deal. So it was a guy by the name Vinnitser and a guy by the name Kac. They said, we willing to pay for you guys. We got the money. We got the gold pieces. We got-- we have the finance.

speaker: interviewee
text: But their, their wives were already gone in the first Aktion. The same Aktion what my mother went, they went. And so they-- but in the meantime, after the Aktion, was like more or less like normal. In the ghetto, the Jews were doing business-- selling, buying, oh, yeah. I wasn't in the ghettos. I don't know. But that's what my father told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- they-- those two-- Kac and that Vinnitser says, we want to see the place. Took him over to the place. He says, we not ready. We still can do business in the ghetto. We know the place, in case something will, will come. So my father-- we dug a little hole underneath the, underneath the bridge-- the, the floor. It's temporary. But then, when they start to talking about liquidation the ghetto, that Kac and Vinnitser took off and came to that Wolszyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, that thing is no good. We've gotta have something permanent built underneath, hidden, concealed, that the Germans won't find. OK. [? Yatsko's ?] house consist of one long dwelling. Was-- the first was the living room, the kitchen, then was a little-- want to call it the den. And then connected to the den was the barn with the horse. He had one horse and two pigs. All you didn't have to go outside to reach from one end to the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that Kac, he by trade, he was an engineer. He says, OK, what are we going do? You see that horse here? Here is the thing where the horse eats, that whatever you want to call it. We'll lift it. We'll dug in inside a corridor about six, seven feet deep. And we'll dig-- the, the, the thing will be outside, underneath.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what he did. Nights, every night, we all dug. We took out the-- he-- the, the, that Wolszyn-- that Jacob, he took the, the earth someplace else so nobody. And we made it. We made it enough to sit down or lay down. But we couldn't stand on our knees. We could sit down. And was a little-- maybe so much-- space left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was four of us. Now, and she was-- the problem was this. Cause he used to take the money and drink it through or give it to the girlfriend. So then we found out, there's anything we want to do [? Yatsko-- ?] there's no use in talk to his wife. We've gotta talk to his girlfriend. She had the whole influence.

speaker: interviewee
text: She used to come in and tell them, oh, you didn't do this. I told you to do it. So she used to lift the hand and hit him all across the face. Oh, yeah. So anyway-- and that's how-- but the woman-- that's where I have the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- she used to say, [? Yatsko, ?] what are you doing? You took all the money. I don't have any money. You have four people. Just let them die, the Jews. He was drunk all the time. That's, that's his-- let them die. How did they-- how did we know? We listened under-- I was listening under the, under the door, behind the door. So anyway, now, another, another system we had, this [? Yatsko ?] he wasn't very bright, you Know? [INAUDIBLE] we told [? Yatsko, ?] we don't have any money on us-- nothing, nothing. You need money, tell us two days before. We'll go out. We connected with somebody. We'll go out. And we'll get the money. He believed that. Why would-- why did we say that? Because why would he keep us and get the money slowly when he could kill us, tell the Germans that he found dead-- you know-- and get, get even a price for it? This way, he knew we don't have any money. And it worked. How did we know it worked? One day, I was listening behind the door. She says, [? Yatsko, ?] the, the dog is barking. He says, the Jews went to get the money. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's-- we moved in there in, in October, 1942. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were there till February, 1944, approximately, a year and a half, till February, '44. Now, during-- when, when we were in the, in hiding, was a city named Brody. [? Yatsko ?] used to go there. And every time we used to-- yeah, one of our [INAUDIBLE], he had a friend in that, in that city. He knew a guy by the name Polak. So he used to-- [? Yatsko ?] used to go. And he used to see that Polak. Polak used to give him papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when that Polak, you know, found out that [? Yatsko ?] was hiding Jews, he knew that, sooner or later in Brody, the, the ghetto will be liquidated. So he start asking questions. [? Yatsko, ?] where is the address? Where do you live? And when it start-- when the, the Germans started liquidating the ghetto, he-- just in the, the daylight-- he came in. What are you going do? We were four. We took him in. We took him in. Took him in, and we were five already. And we were there but-- till, like I said, till February.

speaker: interviewee
text: During-- what-- we didn't depend only on [? Yatsko's ?] food. Because that was-- [? Wygoda ?] was only eight kilometers from Strzemilcze. My father used to do business there. So he used to go out at night, contact farmers. He used to bring food, all kinds of food-- bacon, bread, potatoes. Now, [? Yatsko ?] was very poor. He never had a pair of decent pants. Suddenly, [INAUDIBLE] when he start hiding the Jews, he became wealthy. He got money. So he goes on a, on a market.

speaker: interviewee
text: A guy meets him there-- says, [? Yatsko, ?] what happened? You're dressed so nice. He says, what do you mean? I, I hid-- I am hiding Jews. And they pay me. Oh, you're hiding-- and that guy was an extortionist. So two days later, he came in. Money. My father carried a gun. And he knew how to use it, because he was in the Austrian army. So he said we should never let the guy go-- leave this, the premise.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the rest of the two-- I was-- I didn't have too much to say. But the rest said, no. Give him, this time, the money. We'll tell [? Yatsko. ?] Forget it. They were scared. They got afraid. They left. And that's how it was. We tell him-- we told [? Yatsko, ?] don't ever-- you crazy. They'll kill you. Anyway, he came back. [? Yatsko ?] says, sorry. They were-- they got so scared that they were gone-- never bothered coming back. And that was the end of it. That's an incident we telling. And in '44, in '44, they-- we got liberated by the Russians. And we went back to Strzemilcze, because we had some stuff hidden. My father had jewelry hidden in Strzemilcze. We had some other stuff hidden. And, you know, some-- we had something hidden.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father found it, you know? We had a lot of stuff given to the farmers to hide for us. Half of them didn't give it back. And then I was with my father till May, 1944-- only February, March, April, three months.

speaker: interviewee
text: In May, I got drafted to the army. My father was deadly against it. But I said, I'm going. And my father went west. I went east. And I was in the Russian army from May, '44, till June, '49-- five years, five and change. OK. And during-- in the army, I knew, I knew German. I knew German-- not fluently, because I learned when I worked for them in that grenzschutzkommando in Strzemilcze-- that the border patrol. I learned the language, I'm good on languages.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I was trained by the Russian army. I was trained in engineering-- setting up mines, and taking up mines and taking out. So that was a very dangerous job. So I-- they were looking for interpreter. They hired me. And that's how I lived through the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till-- and then, after the war, I got, I got discharged. Actually, I was in the occupation force in Germany. But when they found out that my father was in Canada, they transferred me to Siberia. It's close to Ural. It's close to Siberia. And there, I got-- I was there six months. And I got discharged. After I got discharged, I didn't have a place where to go. So I went to my-- not to that village. I went to a place called Lopatyn-- L-O-P-A-T-Y-N. That Lopatyn was like the district from our village. Went there, and I start working. I had to work. So I was given a job with [? heights. ?] I didn't have a clue what, what to do. But I learned. I was getting the [? heights ?] from the farmers. Worked for the government, then sort them, then specify them which sort, and sent them back. And I work there for a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Sitzer, I want to go back for a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll get to your postwar experience in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But back to when you were liberated by the, by the Russians-- can you tell us anything more? You were in the military. You were, you were with the Russian army. Were there any experiences--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can tell you a lot, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us any experiences, especially with, with other Jew-- experiences with other Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I can tell you experiences, experiences, which-- like for instance, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK experiences from my services. This-- we-- I blew up a bridge with two other guys. OK? Then, then we got into a house. And we didn't know the Germans were upstairs. And then we start going out. We start-- they started shooting at us. You know, so that was an incident. And then, the Russian-- in that particular unit where I was, was 75% Jews. So it was no anti-Semitism. Actually, I can't say during my service in the Russian army-- very little-- I did, I did a little, but very little anti-Semitism, very little. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter any of the concentration camps or ghettos while you were liberating Poland and Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I run in, in Poland, to a bunch of girls-- Jewish girls. That's-- so you see? You remind me. Ran into a-- to a bunch of Jewish girls. It was-- the city was Cottbus, near Berlin-- 50 kilometers from Berlin. And we got-- I got in-- you know, the, the Germans were running away from the Russians to the American side. They weren't afraid of the Americans like for the Russians. I mean, they had a reason for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got in an apartment building-- me and another guy. And we start looking in that apartment building. There's nobody there. So I got into the basement. In the basement, I see a wall, right? So I see the wall doesn't look real. So it was a false wall. So I took it with my shoulder. And the wall gave, you know? When I opened that wall, was about 150 suitcases-- all kind of suit-- from the whole apartment, you know, building. They packed the suitcases. That was an incident. So we start open it. Took us about two hours to open all the suitcases. Suitcase were all labeled. And when I got out, I met that bunch of girls. And I says, girls, do you need some stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, that's what we're looking for. Took them in, in the basement, those Jewish girls. I says, here, it's all yours. So I don't know. I left, and that's the last of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you encounter any other Jews during any marches or any concentration camps or ghettos? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Because the part I liberated wasn't the part where the concentration camps was, you know? It just wasn't there. Because I went-- I was with that first Ukrainian front, and that was another. What I did in Germany-- I was at, at-- in one time, I had a bunch of people. I was a sergeant. And I was in charge of, you know, behind the front. It's all kind undesirables. I used to check their papers. So we cut some Nazis, some SS people. We used to hand them over. But that's about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with the Nazis when you caught them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just we had orders to bring them in. And what done was later. Some of them, we took over. I remember an incident-- we came in on a farm. Was an empty farmhouse. And when I came in, it smelled human but was nobody there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came in, four or five of us. We start looking. Yeah. So we found albums with pictures. So I see SS with those, you know? I says, somebody must be here. So we start looking. And we found a guy. We found a man. He ran. He was a deserter from the German army. Gave us a line of bull-- you know, does he this. But he was an SS man. He was. He had on here, on the arm. So we didn't bother taking him. We took care of him, you know? So and that's all. You killed him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right there, right there. I remember another incident. You see, it comes back. While I was in that squad, we had long, like, wires-- heavy. We were going looking for deserters, for Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came into one barn. There was hay. We started looking, you know? We found two flyers-- two pilots. Also, you know didn't bother, because I had, I had-- my boss actually gave me permission. He says, you in charge of those people, whatever you think is right. So I used it most of the time. But you know, I, I, I should've take out that picture from my-- from the major. I have it someplace. And that-- actually, in the army, I had it good. Yeah. I had it very good-- can't complain.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was young. And I had always-- you see, then, see, I always had good jobs. I was in charge in the army. You know, the Russians were getting paid for-- from the Germans, like, reparations for, for all the, the Germans, the crimes, the-- for the robberies.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for instance, we had a mill, a mill, which was making-- like a wooden mill, you know-- in a, in a forest. And they were making all kind of material and shipping to Russia. So I was in charge of that, you know? And so they-- you know, the Germans didn't have any material, any boards, any building material. So we used to take care of the material. That's how I used to get my money and, and had a good time. We had girls, and, you know. I had a car in Russia, you know? So at, at this point, where the war's coming to a close. You encountered a lot of Nazis but not many Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What happened to the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They asked-- I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- one of my sisters--

speaker: interviewer
text: The oldest one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest one committed suicide in Germany. The other one didn't arrive. She had Ukrainian papers-- [INAUDIBLE] Don't know what happened. The two other sisters went to Belzec with my mother. And my father survived. Me, me and my father survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father passed away in 19-- in 1987. It was eight years in April-- in Israel. My father came to Canada a lot ahead of me. He came in in '48. I came in '57. So he was already established here, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but till '56-- I left Russia '56, after I gotten-- like in '50-- in '49, I got discharged. I worked. And then I came to Lwow, a city Lemberg. And I met my wife. And I married. Then went looking for work and became-- I worked in different-- always [INAUDIBLE] always a business there. I mean, for the government, I worked. But I was at one time a head of a department store. And then, I had-- on a flea market, on a farmer's market, I had a store. And that's the kind of work. In '56, we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to continue with the story on our next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
